assertions.claim_text: In the late 1950s and early 1960s, the United States was engaged in the Cold War, a geopolitical rivalry with the Soviet Union.
assertions.subject: the United States

assertions.claim_text: A crewed mission to the Moon would serve this purpose.
assertions.subject: A crewed mission

assertions.claim_text: On May 25, 1961, Kennedy addressed the United States Congress on "Urgent National Needs" and declared:
assertions.subject: Kennedy

assertions.claim_text: The initial crew assignment of Commander Neil Armstrong, Command Module Pilot (CMP) Jim Lovell, and Lunar Module Pilot (LMP) Buzz Aldrin on the backup crew for Apollo 9 was officially announced on November 20, 1967.
assertions.subject: The initial crew assignment

assertions.claim_text: Lovell and Aldrin had previously flown together as the crew of Gemini 12.
assertions.subject: Lovell

assertions.claim_text: Due to design and manufacturing delays in the LM, Apollo 8 and Apollo 9 swapped prime and backup crews, and Armstrong's crew became the backup for Apollo 8.
assertions.subject: design and manufacturing delays

assertions.claim_text: Based on the normal crew rotation scheme, Armstrong was then expected to command Apollo 11.
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: There would be one change.
Michael Collins, the CMP on the Apollo 8 crew, began experiencing trouble with his legs.
assertions.subject: Michael Collins

assertions.claim_text: Lovell took his place on the Apollo 8 crew, and when Collins recovered he joined Armstrong's crew as CMP.
assertions.subject: Lovell

assertions.claim_text: In the meantime, Fred Haise filled in as backup LMP, and Aldrin as backup CMP for Apollo 8.
assertions.subject: Fred Haise

assertions.claim_text: Apollo 11 was the second American mission where all the crew members had prior spaceflight experience, the first being Apollo 10.
assertions.subject: Apollo

assertions.claim_text: The next was STS-26 in 1988.
assertions.subject: next

assertions.claim_text: Deke Slayton gave Armstrong the option to replace Aldrin with Lovell, since some thought Aldrin was difficult to work with.
assertions.subject: Deke Slayton

assertions.claim_text: Armstrong had no issues working with Aldrin but thought it over for a day before declining.
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: He thought Lovell deserved to command his own mission (eventually Apollo 13).
assertions.subject: He

assertions.claim_text: The Apollo 11 prime crew had none of the close cheerful camaraderie characterized by that of Apollo 12.
assertions.subject: The Apollo 11 prime crew

assertions.claim_text: Instead, they forged an amiable working relationship.
assertions.subject: they

assertions.claim_text: Armstrong in particular was notoriously aloof, but Collins, who considered himself a loner, confessed to rebuffing Aldrin's attempts to create a more personal relationship.
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: Aldrin and Collins described the crew as "amiable strangers".
assertions.subject: Aldrin

assertions.claim_text: Armstrong did not agree with the assessment, and said "all the crews I was on worked very well together.
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text:
"

The backup crew consisted of Lovell as Commander, William Anders as CMP, and Haise as LMP.
assertions.subject: The backup crew

assertions.claim_text: Anders had flown with Lovell on Apollo 8.
assertions.subject: Anders

assertions.claim_text: Ken Mattingly was moved from the support crew into parallel training with Anders as backup CMP in case Apollo 11 was delayed past its intended July launch date, at which point Anders would be unavailable.
assertions.subject: Ken Mattingly

assertions.claim_text: During Projects Mercury and Gemini, each mission had a prime and a backup crew.
assertions.subject: each mission

assertions.claim_text: The flight directors for this mission were:
assertions.subject: The flight directors

assertions.claim_text: Other key personnel who played important roles in the Apollo 11 mission include the following.
assertions.subject: REF

assertions.claim_text: The Apollo 11 mission emblem was designed by Collins, who wanted a symbol for "peaceful lunar landing by the United States".
assertions.subject: The Apollo 11 mission emblem

assertions.claim_text: At Lovell's suggestion, he chose the bald eagle, the national bird of the United States, as the symbol.
assertions.subject: he

assertions.claim_text: Tom Wilson, a simulator instructor, suggested an olive branch in its beak to represent their peaceful mission.
assertions.subject: Tom Wilson

assertions.claim_text: Collins added a lunar background with the Earth in the distance.
assertions.subject: Collins

assertions.claim_text: The sunlight in the image was coming from the wrong direction; the shadow should have been in the lower part of the Earth instead of the left.
assertions.subject: the shadow

assertions.claim_text: Aldrin, Armstrong and Collins decided the Eagle and the Moon would be in their natural colors, and decided on a blue and gold border.
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: Armstrong was concerned that "eleven" would not be understood by non-English speakers, so they went with "Apollo 11", and they decided not to put their names on the patch, so it would "be representative of everyone who had worked toward a lunar landing".
assertions.subject: they

assertions.claim_text: An illustrator at the Manned Spacecraft Center (MSC) did the artwork, which was then sent off to NASA officials for approval.
assertions.subject: An illustrator

assertions.claim_text: The design was rejected.
assertions.subject: The design

assertions.claim_text: Bob Gilruth, the director of the MSC felt the talons of the eagle looked "too warlike".
assertions.subject: Bob Gilruth

assertions.claim_text: After some discussion, the olive branch was moved to the talons.
assertions.subject: the olive branch

assertions.claim_text: When the Eisenhower dollar coin was released in 1971, the patch design provided the eagle for its reverse side.
assertions.subject: the patch design

assertions.claim_text: The name Snowcone was used for the CM and Haystack was used for the LM in both internal and external communications during early mission planning.
assertions.subject: The name

assertions.claim_text: The LM was named Eagle after the motif which was featured prominently on the mission insignia.
assertions.subject: The LM

assertions.claim_text: At Scheer's suggestion, the CM was named Columbia after Columbiad, the giant cannon that launched a spacecraft (also from Florida) in Jules Verne's 1865 novel From the Earth to the Moon.
assertions.subject: the CM

assertions.claim_text: It also referred to Columbia, a historical name of the United States.
assertions.subject: It

assertions.claim_text: In Collins' 1976 book, he said Columbia was in reference to Christopher Columbus.
assertions.subject: he

assertions.claim_text:
]]

The astronauts had personal preference kits (PPKs), small bags containing personal items of significance they wanted to take with them on the mission.
assertions.subject: The astronauts

assertions.claim_text: NASA's Apollo Site Selection Board announced five potential landing sites on February 8, 1968.
assertions.subject: NASA's Apollo Site Selection Board

assertions.claim_text: These were the result of two years' worth of studies based on high-resolution photography of the lunar surface by the five uncrewed probes of the Lunar Orbiter program and information about surface conditions provided by the Surveyor program.
assertions.subject: These

assertions.claim_text: The best Earth-bound telescopes could not resolve features with the resolution Project Apollo required.
assertions.subject: The best Earth-bound telescopes

assertions.claim_text: The landing site had to be close to the lunar equator to minimize the amount of propellant required, clear of obstacles to minimize maneuvering, and flat to simplify the task of the landing radar.
assertions.subject: The landing site

assertions.claim_text: Scientific value was not a consideration.
assertions.subject: Scientific value

assertions.claim_text: Areas that appeared promising on photographs taken on Earth were often found to be totally unacceptable.
assertions.subject: Areas

assertions.claim_text: The original requirement that the site be free of craters had to be relaxed, as no such site was found.
assertions.subject: The original requirement

assertions.claim_text:
The final site selection was based on seven criteria:
*
assertions.subject: The final site selection
one

assertions.claim_text: The requirement for the Sun angle was particularly restrictive, limiting the launch date to one day per month.
assertions.subject: The requirement

assertions.claim_text: A landing just after dawn was chosen to limit the temperature extremes the astronauts would experience.
assertions.subject: A landing

assertions.claim_text: The Apollo Site Selection Board selected Site 2, with Sites 3 and 5 as backups in the event of the launch being delayed.
assertions.subject: The Apollo Site Selection Board

assertions.claim_text: In May 1969, Apollo 10's lunar module flew to within of Site 2, and reported it was acceptable.
assertions.subject: Apollo 10's lunar module

assertions.claim_text: During the first press conference after the Apollo 11 crew was announced, the first question was, "Which one of you gentlemen will be the first man to step onto the lunar surface?"
assertions.subject: the first question

assertions.claim_text: Slayton told the reporter it had not been decided, and Armstrong added that it was "not based on individual desire".
assertions.subject: Slayton

assertions.claim_text: One of the first versions of the egress checklist had the lunar module pilot exit the spacecraft before the commander, which matched what had been done on Gemini missions, where the commander had never performed the spacewalk.
assertions.subject: One

assertions.claim_text: Reporters wrote in early 1969 that Aldrin would be the first man to walk on the Moon, and Associate Administrator George Mueller told reporters he would be first as well.
assertions.subject: Reporters

assertions.claim_text: Aldrin heard that Armstrong would be the first because Armstrong was a civilian, which made Aldrin livid.
assertions.subject: Aldrin

assertions.claim_text: Aldrin attempted to persuade other Lunar Module pilots he should be first, but they responded cynically about what they perceived as a lobbying campaign.
assertions.subject: Aldrin

assertions.claim_text: Attempting to stem interdepartmental conflict, Slayton told Aldrin that Armstrong would be first since he was the commander.
assertions.subject: Slayton

assertions.claim_text: The decision was announced in a press conference on April 14, 1969.
assertions.subject: The decision

assertions.claim_text: For decades, Aldrin believed the final decision was largely driven by the Lunar Module's hatch location.
assertions.subject: Aldrin

assertions.claim_text: Because the astronauts had their spacesuits on and the spacecraft was so small, maneuvering to exit the spacecraft was difficult.
assertions.subject: the astronauts

assertions.claim_text: The crew tried a simulation in which Aldrin left the spacecraft first, but he damaged the simulator while attempting to egress.
assertions.subject: The crew

assertions.claim_text: While this was enough for mission planners to make their decision, Aldrin and Armstrong were left in the dark on the decision until late spring.
assertions.subject: Aldrin

assertions.claim_text: Slayton told Armstrong the plan was to have him leave the spacecraft first, if he agreed.
assertions.subject: Slayton

assertions.claim_text: Armstrong said, "Yes, that's the way to do it."
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: The media accused Armstrong of exercising his commander's prerogative to exit the spacecraft first.
assertions.subject: The media

assertions.claim_text: Chris Kraft revealed in his 2001 autobiography that a meeting occurred between Gilruth, Slayton, Low, and himself to make sure Aldrin would not be the first to walk on the Moon.
assertions.subject: Chris Kraft

assertions.claim_text: They argued that the first person to walk on the Moon should be like Charles Lindbergh, a calm and quiet person.
assertions.subject: They

assertions.claim_text: They made the decision to change the flight plan so the commander was the first to egress from the spacecraft.
assertions.subject: They

assertions.claim_text:
towards Launch Complex 39.]]

The ascent stage of LM-5 Eagle arrived at the Kennedy Space Center on January 8, 1969, followed by the descent stage four days later, and CSM-107 Columbia on January 23.
assertions.subject: The ascent stage

assertions.claim_text: There were several differences between Eagle and Apollo 10's LM-4

Snoopy; Eagle had a VHF radio antenna to facilitate communication with the astronauts during their EVA on the lunar surface; a lighter ascent engine; more thermal protection on the landing gear; and a package of scientific experiments known as the Early Apollo Scientific Experiments Package (EASEP).
assertions.subject: Eagle

assertions.claim_text: The only change in the configuration of the command module was the removal of some insulation from the forward hatch.
assertions.subject: The only change

assertions.claim_text: An estimated one million spectators watched the launch of Apollo 11 from the highways and beaches in the vicinity of the launch site.
assertions.subject: An estimated one million spectators

assertions.claim_text: Dignitaries included the Chief of Staff of the United States Army, General William Westmoreland, four cabinet members, 19 state governors, 40 mayors, 60 ambassadors and 200 congressmen.
assertions.subject: Dignitaries

assertions.claim_text: Vice President Spiro Agnew viewed the launch with former president Lyndon B. Johnson and his wife Lady Bird Johnson.
assertions.subject: Vice President Spiro Agnew

assertions.claim_text: Around 3,500 media representatives were present.
assertions.subject: Around 3,500 media representatives

assertions.claim_text: About two-thirds were from the United States; the rest came from 55 other countries.
assertions.subject: the rest

assertions.claim_text: The launch was televised live in 33 countries, with an estimated 25 million viewers in the United States alone.
assertions.subject: The launch

assertions.claim_text: Millions more around the world listened to radio broadcasts.
assertions.subject: Millions

assertions.claim_text: President Richard Nixon viewed the launch from his office in the White House with his NASA liaison officer, Apollo astronaut Frank Borman.
assertions.subject: President Richard Nixon

assertions.claim_text:
in lunar orbit, photographed from Eagle|alt=The top of the silvery command module is seen over a grey, cratered lunar surface]]

At 12:52:00 UTC on July 20, Aldrin and Armstrong entered Eagle, and began the final preparations for lunar descent.
assertions.subject: Aldrin

assertions.claim_text: At 17:44:00 Eagle separated from Columbia.
assertions.subject: Eagle

assertions.claim_text: Collins, alone aboard Columbia, inspected Eagle as it pirouetted before him to ensure the craft was not damaged, and that the landing gear was correctly deployed.
assertions.subject: Collins

assertions.claim_text: Armstrong exclaimed: "The Eagle has wings!"
assertions.subject: The Eagle

assertions.claim_text: As the descent began, Armstrong and Aldrin found themselves passing landmarks on the surface two or three seconds early, and reported that they were "long"; they would land miles west of their target point.
assertions.subject: they

assertions.claim_text: Eagle was traveling too fast.
assertions.subject: Eagle

assertions.claim_text: The problem could have been mascons—concentrations of high mass in a region or regions of the Moon's crust that contains a gravitational anomaly, potentially altering Eagle trajectory.
assertions.subject: The problem

assertions.claim_text: Flight Director Gene Kranz speculated that it could have resulted from extra air pressure in the docking tunnel, or a result of Eagles pirouette maneuver.
assertions.subject: Flight Director Gene Kranz

assertions.claim_text: Five minutes into the descent burn, and above the surface of the Moon, the LM guidance computer (LGC) distracted the crew with the first of several unexpected 1201 and 1202 program alarms.
assertions.subject: the LM guidance computer

assertions.claim_text: Inside Mission Control Center, computer engineer Jack Garman told Guidance Officer Steve Bales it was safe to continue the descent, and this was relayed to the crew.
assertions.subject: computer engineer Jack Garman

assertions.claim_text: The program alarms indicated "executive overflows", meaning the guidance computer could not complete all its tasks in real-time and had to postpone some of them.
assertions.subject: The program alarms

assertions.claim_text:
Margaret Hamilton, the Director of Apollo Flight Computer Programming at the MIT Charles Stark Draper Laboratory later recalled:
 in lunar orbit photographed from Columbia]]
Armstrong considered landing short of the boulder field so they could collect geological samples from it, but could not since their horizontal velocity was too high.
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: Throughout the descent, Aldrin called out navigation data to Armstrong, who was busy piloting Eagle.
assertions.subject: Aldrin

assertions.claim_text: Now above the surface, Armstrong knew their propellant supply was dwindling and was determined to land at the first possible landing site.
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: Armstrong found a clear patch of ground and maneuvered the spacecraft towards it.
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: As he got closer, now above the surface, he discovered his new landing site had a crater in it.
assertions.subject: he

assertions.claim_text: He cleared the crater and found another patch of level ground.
assertions.subject: He

assertions.claim_text: They were now from the surface, with only 90 seconds of propellant remaining.
assertions.subject: They

assertions.claim_text: Lunar dust kicked up by the LM's engine began to impair his ability to determine the spacecraft's motion.
assertions.subject: Lunar dust

assertions.claim_text: Some large rocks jutted out of the dust cloud, and Armstrong focused on them during his descent so he could determine the spacecraft's speed.
assertions.subject: Some large rocks

assertions.claim_text: A light informed Aldrin that at least one of the probes hanging from Eagle footpads had touched the surface a few moments before the landing and he said: "Contact light!"
assertions.subject: A light

assertions.claim_text: Armstrong was supposed to immediately shut the engine down, as the engineers suspected the pressure caused by the engine's own exhaust reflecting off the lunar surface could make it explode, but he forgot.
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: Three seconds later, Eagle landed and Armstrong shut the engine down.
assertions.subject: Eagle

assertions.claim_text: Aldrin immediately said "Okay, engine stop.
assertions.subject: Aldrin

assertions.claim_text: Armstrong acknowledged: "Out of detent.
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: Aldrin continued: "Mode control—both auto.
assertions.subject: Aldrin

assertions.claim_text: Descent engine command override off.
assertions.subject: Descent engine command
ACA

assertions.claim_text: Output went to the LGC to command the reaction control system (RCS) jets to fire.
assertions.subject: Output

assertions.claim_text: "Out of Detent" meant the stick had moved away from its centered position; it was spring-centered like the turn indicator in a car.
assertions.subject: it

assertions.claim_text: Eagle landed at 20:17:40 UTC on Sunday July 20 with of usable fuel remaining.
assertions.subject: Eagle

assertions.claim_text:
Information available to the crew and mission controllers during the landing showed the LM had enough fuel for another 25 seconds of powered flight before an abort without touchdown would have become unsafe,

Armstrong acknowledged Aldrin's completion of the post-landing checklist with "Engine arm is off", before responding to the CAPCOM, Charles Duke, with the words, "Houston, Tranquility Base here.
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: The Eagle has landed."
assertions.subject: The Eagle

assertions.claim_text: Armstrong's unrehearsed change of call sign from "Eagle" to "Tranquility Base" emphasized to listeners that landing was complete and successful.
assertions.subject: Armstrong's unrehearsed change

assertions.claim_text: These took longer than expected; three and a half hours instead of two.
assertions.subject: These

assertions.claim_text: During training on Earth, everything required had been neatly laid out in advance, but on the Moon the cabin contained a large number of other items as well, such as checklists, food packets, and tools.
assertions.subject: everything

assertions.claim_text: Six hours and thirty-nine minutes after landing, Armstrong and Aldrin were ready to go outside, and Eagle was depressurized.
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: Eagles hatch was opened at 02:39:33.
assertions.subject: Eagles hatch

assertions.claim_text: Armstrong initially had some difficulties squeezing through the hatch with his portable life support system (PLSS).
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: Some of the highest heart rates recorded from Apollo astronauts occurred during LM egress and ingress.
assertions.subject: Some

assertions.claim_text: At 02:51 Armstrong began his descent to the lunar surface.
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: Climbing down the nine-rung ladder, Armstrong pulled a D-ring to deploy the modular equipment stowage assembly (MESA) folded against Eagle side and activate the TV camera.
assertions.subject: Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: Aldrin entered Eagle first.
assertions.subject: Aldrin

assertions.claim_text: With some difficulty the astronauts lifted film and two sample boxes containing of lunar surface material to the LM hatch using a flat cable pulley device called the Lunar Equipment Conveyor (LEC).
assertions.subject: the astronauts

assertions.claim_text: This proved to be an inefficient tool, and later missions preferred to carry equipment and samples up to the LM by hand.
assertions.subject: This

assertions.claim_text: During his day flying solo around the Moon, Collins never felt lonely.
assertions.subject: Collins

assertions.claim_text: Although it has been said "not since Adam has any human known such solitude", Collins felt very much a part of the mission.
assertions.subject: Collins

assertions.claim_text: In his autobiography he wrote: "this venture has been structured for three men, and I consider my third to be as necessary as either of the other two".
assertions.subject: he

assertions.claim_text: In the 48 minutes of each orbit when he was out of radio contact with the Earth while Columbia passed round the far side of the Moon, the feeling he reported was not fear or loneliness, but rather "awareness, anticipation, satisfaction, confidence, almost exultation".
assertions.subject: the feeling

assertions.claim_text: One of Collins' first tasks was to identify the Lunar Module on the ground.
assertions.subject: One

assertions.claim_text: To give Collins an idea where to look, Mission Control radioed that they believed the Lunar Module landed about off target.
assertions.subject: Mission Control

assertions.claim_text: Each time he passed over the suspected lunar landing site, he tried in vain to find the module.
assertions.subject: he

assertions.claim_text: On his first orbits on the back side of the Moon, Collins performed maintenance activities such as dumping excess water produced by the fuel cells and preparing the cabin for Armstrong and Aldrin to return.
assertions.subject: Collins

assertions.claim_text: Just before he reached the dark side on the third orbit, Mission Control informed Collins there was a problem with the temperature of the coolant.
assertions.subject: Mission Control

assertions.claim_text: If it became too cold, parts of Columbia might freeze.
assertions.subject: parts

assertions.claim_text: Mission Control advised him to assume manual control and implement Environmental Control System Malfunction Procedure 17.
assertions.subject: Mission Control

assertions.claim_text: Instead, Collins flicked the switch on the system from automatic to manual and back to automatic again, and carried on with normal housekeeping chores, while keeping an eye on the temperature.
assertions.subject: Collins

assertions.claim_text: When Columbia came back around to the near side of the Moon again, he was able to report that the problem had been resolved.
assertions.subject: he

assertions.claim_text: After Aldrin and Armstrong completed their EVA, Collins slept so he could be rested for the rendezvous.
assertions.subject: Collins

assertions.claim_text: While the flight plan called for Eagle to meet up with Columbia, Collins was prepared for a contingency in which he would fly Columbia down to meet Eagle.
assertions.subject: Collins

assertions.claim_text:
ascent stage approaching Columbia]]
Eagle rendezvoused with Columbia at 21:24 UTC on July 21, and the two docked at 21:35.
assertions.subject: ascent stage

assertions.claim_text: Eagles ascent stage was jettisoned into lunar orbit at 23:41.
assertions.subject: Eagles ascent stage

assertions.claim_text: Just before the Apollo 12 flight, it was noted that Eagle was still likely to be orbiting the Moon.
assertions.subject: it

assertions.claim_text: Later NASA reports mentioned that Eagle orbit had decayed, resulting in it impacting in an "uncertain location" on the lunar surface.
assertions.subject: reports

assertions.claim_text: Hornet was then at her home port of Long Beach, California.
assertions.subject: Hornet

assertions.claim_text: On reaching Pearl Harbor on July 5, Hornet embarked the Sikorsky SH-3 Sea King helicopters of HS-4, a unit which specialized in recovery of Apollo spacecraft, specialized divers of UDT Detachment Apollo, a 35-man NASA recovery team, and about 120 media representatives.
assertions.subject: Hornet

assertions.claim_text: To make room, most of Hornets air wing was left behind in Long Beach.
assertions.subject: most

assertions.claim_text: Special recovery equipment was also loaded, including a boilerplate command module used for training.
assertions.subject: Special recovery equipment

assertions.claim_text: On July 12, with Apollo 11 still on the launch pad, Hornet departed Pearl Harbor for the recovery area in the central Pacific, in the vicinity of .
assertions.subject: Hornet

assertions.claim_text:
newspaper stating The Eagle Has Landed' – Two Men Walk on the Moon"]]
Humans walking on the Moon and returning safely to Earth accomplished Kennedy's goal set eight years earlier.
assertions.subject: newspaper

assertions.claim_text: In Mission Control during the Apollo 11 landing, Kennedy's speech flashed on the screen, followed by the words "TASK ACCOMPLISHED, July 1969".
assertions.subject: Kennedy's speech

assertions.claim_text: The success of Apollo 11 demonstrated the United States' technological superiority; and with the success of Apollo 11, America had won the Space Race.
assertions.subject: The success

assertions.claim_text: New phrases permeated into the English language.
assertions.subject: New phrases

assertions.claim_text: "If they can send a man to the Moon, why can't they ...?" became a common saying following Apollo 11.

on display in the Milestones of Flight exhibition hall at the National Air and Space Museum]]

The command module Columbia went on a tour of the United States, visiting 49 state capitals, the District of Columbia, and Anchorage, Alaska.
assertions.subject: The command module

assertions.claim_text: The main repository for the Apollo Moon rocks is the Lunar Sample Laboratory Facility at the Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center in Houston, Texas.
assertions.subject: The main repository

assertions.claim_text: For safekeeping, there is also a smaller collection stored at White Sands Test Facility near Las Cruces, New Mexico.
Most of the rocks are stored in nitrogen to keep them free of moisture.
assertions.subject: Most

assertions.claim_text: They are handled only indirectly, using special tools.
assertions.subject: They

assertions.claim_text: Over 100 research laboratories worldwide conduct studies of the samples; approximately 500 samples are prepared and sent to investigators every year.
assertions.subject: approximately 500 samples

assertions.claim_text: The Passive Seismic Experiment ran until the command uplink failed on August 25, 1969.
assertions.subject: The Passive Seismic Experiment

assertions.claim_text: The downlink failed on December 14, 1969.
assertions.subject: The downlink

assertions.claim_text: , the Lunar Laser Ranging experiment remains operational.
assertions.subject: the Lunar Laser Ranging experiment

assertions.claim_text: In 2015, after Armstrong died in 2012, his widow contacted the National Air and Space Museum to inform them she had found a white cloth bag in one of Armstrong's closets.
assertions.subject: his widow

assertions.claim_text:
"40th anniversary events">40th anniversary ====

On July 15, 2009, Life.com released a photo gallery of previously unpublished photos of the astronauts taken by Life photographer Ralph Morse prior to the Apollo 11 launch.

On June 10, 2015, Congressman Bill Posey introduced resolution H.R. 2726 to the 114th session of the United States House of Representatives directing the United States Mint to design and sell commemorative coins in gold, silver and clad for the 50th anniversary of the Apollo 11 mission.
assertions.subject: Congressman Bill Posey

assertions.claim_text: On January 24, 2019, the Mint released the Apollo 11 Fiftieth Anniversary commemorative coins to the public on its website.
assertions.subject: the Mint

assertions.claim_text: The aircraft carrier , under the command of Captain Carl J. Seiberlich, was selected as the primary recovery ship (PRS) for Apollo 11 on June 5, replacing her sister ship, the LPH , which had recovered Apollo 10 on May 26.
assertions.subject: The aircraft carrier

assertions.claim_text: Preparations for Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin to walk on the Moon began at 23:43 UTC.
assertions.subject: Preparations

assertions.claim_text: Five sites were considered: Sites 1 and 2 were in the Sea of Tranquility (Mare Tranquillitatis); Site 3 was in the Central Bay (); and Sites 4 and 5 were in the Ocean of Storms (Oceanus Procellarum).
assertions.subject: Site

assertions.claim_text: When Armstrong again looked outside, he saw that the computer's landing system was targeting a boulder-strewn area just north and east of a crater (later determined to be West crater), so he took semi-automatic control.
assertions.subject: he

assertions.claim_text: The bag contained various items, which should have been left behind in the Lunar Module Eagle, including the 16mm Data Acquisition Camera that had been used to capture images of the first Moon landing.
assertions.subject: The bag

assertions.claim_text: Doctors diagnosed a bony growth between his fifth and sixth vertebrae, requiring surgery.
assertions.subject: Doctors

assertions.claim_text: President Dwight D. Eisenhower responded to the challenge posed by Sputnik by creating the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), and initiating Project Mercury, which aimed to place a man into Earth orbit.
assertions.subject: President Dwight D. Eisenhower

assertions.claim_text:
, the very high resolution image was produced in 2007 by the Smithsonian Institution.]]
After the crew of Apollo 10 named their spacecraft Charlie Brown and Snoopy, assistant manager for public affairs Julian Scheer wrote to George Low, the Manager of the Apollo Spacecraft Program Office at the MSC, to suggest the Apollo 11 crew be less flippant in naming their craft.
assertions.subject: Julian Scheer

assertions.claim_text:
(left), with backup crewmembers Lovell and Haise listening in during Apollo 11's descent]]

The capsule communicator (CAPCOM) was an astronaut stationed at the Mission Control Center in Houston, Texas, and was typically the only person permitted to communicate directly with the flight crew.
assertions.subject: The capsule communicator

assertions.claim_text: The support crew for Apollo 11 was Mattingly, Ronald Evans, and Bill Pogue.
assertions.subject: The support crew

assertions.claim_text: On October 4, 1957, the Soviet Union launched Sputnik 1, the first artificial satellite, surprising the world and fueling fears about Soviet technological and military capabilities.
assertions.subject: the Soviet Union

assertions.claim_text: Under the normal Apollo crew rotation, Lovell, Mattingly, and Haise would have been assigned to Apollo 14.
assertions.subject: Haise

assertions.claim_text: In early 1969, Anders accepted a job with the National Aeronautics and Space Council effective August 1969, and announced he would retire as an astronaut at that time.
assertions.subject: Anders

assertions.claim_text: Address 413 of the Abort Guidance System (AGS) contained the variable that indicated the LM had landed.
assertions.subject: Address

Presidential speech writer William Safire had prepared an In Event of Moon Disaster announcement for Nixon to read in the event the Apollo 11 astronauts were stranded on the Moon.
assertions.subject: Eagle

assertions.claim_text: The Hasselblad camera used during the moonwalk was thought to be lost or left on the Moon's surface.
assertions.subject: The Hasselblad camera

assertions.claim_text: Its success demonstrated that the USSR could potentially deliver nuclear weapons over intercontinental distances, challenging American claims of military, economic, and technological superiority.
assertions.subject: Its success

assertions.claim_text: This incident sparked the Sputnik crisis and ignited the Space Race, as both superpowers sought to demonstrate superiority in spaceflight.
assertions.subject: This incident

assertions.claim_text: The Soviets took the lead on April 12, 1961, when cosmonaut Yuri Gagarin became the first person in space and the first to orbit the Earth.
assertions.subject: The Soviets

assertions.claim_text: Nearly a month later, on May 5, 1961, Alan Shepard became the first American in space; his 15-minute flight was suborbital, not a full orbit.
assertions.subject: his 15-minute flight

assertions.claim_text: Because the Soviet Union had launch vehicles with higher lift capacity, President John F. Kennedy, Eisenhower's successor, chose a challenge that exceeded the capabilities of the existing generation of rockets, so that both countries would be starting from an equal position.
assertions.subject: President John F. Kennedy

assertions.claim_text: However, because Alan Shepard had only recently returned to flight status and needed additional training, NASA reassigned his crew to Apollo 14 and moved Lovell's crew up to fly Apollo 13 instead.
assertions.subject: NASA

assertions.claim_text: Shortly before launch, Mattingly was replaced by Jack Swigert due to concerns over possible measles exposure.
assertions.subject: Mattingly

assertions.claim_text: For Apollo, NASA added a third group, the support crew.
assertions.subject: NASA

assertions.claim_text: Their job was to maintain the flight plan, checklists, and mission rules, and to keep the prime and backup crews updated on any changes.
assertions.subject: Their job

assertions.claim_text: They also developed procedures—especially for emergencies, so that by the time the prime and backup crews entered the simulators, they could focus on practicing and mastering them.
assertions.subject: They

assertions.claim_text: This practice ensured that messages came from someone best able to understand the situation in the spacecraft and to relay guidance from flight controllers as clearly as possible.
assertions.subject: This practice

assertions.claim_text: For Apollo 11, the CAPCOMs were backup and support crew members Anders, Evans, Haise, Lovell, and Mattingly, along with Charles Duke, Owen Garriott, Don L. Lind, Bruce McCandless II, and Harrison Schmitt.
assertions.subject: the CAPCOMs

assertions.claim_text: The remote-control unit on his chest kept him from seeing his feet.
assertions.subject: The remote-control unit

assertions.claim_text: Following their return to Earth, the Apollo 11 crew received widespread international acclaim.
assertions.subject: the Apollo 11 crew

assertions.claim_text: On August 13, 1969, astronauts Neil Armstrong, Buzz Aldrin, and Michael Collins were honored with ticker-tape parades in New York City and Chicago, with an estimated six million people lining the streets.
assertions.subject: astronauts Neil Armstrong

assertions.claim_text: That evening, a state dinner was held in Los Angeles at the Century Plaza Hotel to commemorate the historic achievement.
assertions.subject: a state dinner

assertions.claim_text: The event was attended by members of Congress, 44 state governors, Chief Justice of the United States Warren E. Burger and his predecessor Earl Warren, as well as ambassadors from 83 nations.
assertions.subject: The event

assertions.claim_text: President Richard Nixon and Vice President Spiro Agnew awarded each astronaut the Presidential Medal of Freedom, the highest civilian honor in the United States.
assertions.subject: President Richard Nixon

assertions.claim_text: On September 16, the astronauts addressed a joint session of Congress, where they presented two American flags—one to the House of Representatives and one to the Senate—that had traveled to the lunar surface aboard Eagle.
assertions.subject: REF

assertions.claim_text: One of the mission flags carried aboard the flight—the Flag of American Samoa—was later displayed at the Jean P. Haydon Museum in Pago Pago, the capital of American Samoa, recognizing the territory's support role as a recovery staging area.
assertions.subject: REF

assertions.claim_text:

The celebrations continued with a 38-day world goodwill tour titled "Giant Leap", which began on September 29 and concluded on November 5, 1969.
assertions.subject: REF

assertions.claim_text: The astronauts visited 22 countries and met with numerous heads of state, prime ministers, royalty, and civic leaders.
assertions.subject: The astronauts

assertions.claim_text: The tour, intended to thank the international community for their support of the space program, began in Mexico City and ended in Tokyo.
assertions.subject: The tour

assertions.claim_text: Notable stops included Bogotá, Buenos Aires, Rio de Janeiro, Madrid, Paris, Amsterdam, Brussels, London, Rome, Belgrade, Tehran, Mumbai, Bangkok, Sydney, Guam, and Honolulu.
assertions.subject: Notable stops

assertions.claim_text: Crowds in the tens or hundreds of thousands gathered to greet the astronauts in each city.
assertions.subject: REF:tour-itinerary] Crowds

assertions.claim_text: Numerous countries and organizations honored the Moon landing by issuing special commemorative items.
assertions.subject: Numerous countries

assertions.claim_text: These included postage stamps, coins, medals, plaques, and magazine features.
assertions.subject: These

assertions.claim_text: TIME, National Geographic, LIFE, and dozens of international publications featured the astronauts on their covers.
assertions.subject: TIME

assertions.claim_text: Many of these commemoratives are now held in public and private collections, and some were placed aboard later Apollo missions in symbolic tribute.
assertions.subject: Many

assertions.claim_text: Additionally, the success of Apollo 11 contributed to a brief spike in interest in science and technology education, often referred to as the “Apollo effect,” influencing a generation of engineers and scientists.
assertions.subject: REF
